You overcome a condition with which you are afflicted, whether by resisting it or recovering from it over time. It may be physical, like a broken bone, a poison, or a disease; mental, such as guilt, pain, psychic control, resurfaced emotions, or a recurrence of madness; or a combination of both, such as torture.
This action is often taken while camping or sojourning.

negative: physical or mental issues that keep you from functioning, such as physical weakness or despair
the source of the condition or issue
dark or demonic forces eroding your psyche

entries.success:
LESSEN incoming statuses or story tags of a condition when you are first afflicted by it.
RESTORE to remove a debilitating tag or status when you take action to recover from a condition.
BESTOW or ENHANCE to give yourself tags and statuses that represent temporarily-elevated constitution or newly-gained strength, such as poison resistance, steadfast heart, tamed emotions, stalwart frame, or merry-disposition, full-of-vigor, or hale-of-health.
extraFeats: Recover faster than normal.
Ignore the next Threat to afflict you with the same source (venom, spell, etc.)
consequences: You suffer from further injuries (get a different status or negative tag; only if there was no Success).
You are weary from the healing process (exhausted or scratch tags).
You get a foul-mood due to the long healing period, embarrassed by the malady, angry at the gods, or argumentative with the healers.

entries.name: Treating Injuries and Other Conditions
actionExamples: Perform chirurgy with a hot knife
entries.explanation:
You use your abilities to treat a harmful condition such as an injury or a personal issue. While some issues can be treated in the field or on the job, other, more stubborn problems may require someone with healing powers, extended rest and care, or a period of training to adapt to one's new condition; see Mental and Physical Resilience and Recovery (page 52).
See also Healing with Magic (page 68).
A harmful status or tag being removed does not count as negative for this action, unless you are treating yourself or it is particularly aggressive or stubborn.
When using this action to discover the source of an ailment, bear in mind that some causes might seem merely superstitious, such as a demon or witch's curse. The magical rules of your setting determine what's reasonable, but the successful use of this action should reveal actual causes, not just convenient scapegoats.
This action is often taken while camping or sojourning.
entries.success:
RESTORE to remove the harmful tag or status.
DISCOVER to understand the ailment better or pinpoint its source.
extraFeats: Complete the treatment faster or allow for a speedy recovery.
Leave no scars or treatment side-effects.
Prevent wounds from reopening, for now.
Prevent infections, nightmares, etc.
Move the patient while treatment is ongoing.
Prevent the condition from spreading to others.
Prevent the condition from doing more harm (e.g., ongoing poison).
consequences: The patient's state deteriorates or they suffer from further injuries (increase status or add tags; only if there was no Success).
Your method of treatment proves ineffective (Blocked; only if there was no Success).
The treatment takes a lot of supplies, such as wrappings or medicinal herbs to treat (scratch a tag or short-on-supplies).
You are overworked (frustrated, tired, or distracted).
The illness spreads to you (gain the status afflicting them).

entries.name: Dispelling Magic
entries.explanation:
You use your magical powers or arcane knowledge to stop a magic power from activating, nullify an existing one, or dampen magical ability.
In some cases the Narrator can reintroduce a magical power that was canceled as Consequences. Challenges, in particular, are likely to be able to restore lost abilities.
Consider Might when attempting to nullify the magic of much greater or weaker magic workers, or one affecting a large area.
When dispelling as a reaction, if your rolled total is 10 or higher, you can use some of your Power to learn this spell (Create), absorb its magical energy (Enhance), or reflect it back to the attacker or to another target (Attack), depending on the rules of magic in your setting.

positive: knowledge of, or power over, some supernatural forces
abilities or attitude that perfectly counter the target ability
the opposite element to what's being countered (earth vs. air, fire vs. water, etc.).
negative: the target's magical skills or force of spirit
the raw power of the effect
magical nexuses or ley lines reinforcing existing magic

entries.success:
WEAKEN or RESTORE to remove tags and statuses representing magic or originating from them, such as door-binding spell, giant's strength, warded, or cursed.
DISRUPT the target with statuses that hinder the use of magic powers such as dampened-magic, cut-off-from-the-divine, or muted. These are treated as helpful when lessening the Consequences they're affecting.
LESSEN to reduce Effects of a power just as it is being activated.
extraFeats: Entirely block the target from using a specific ability, for now.
Keep the spotlight to act immediately.
Avoid magical backlash.
Counter or dispel magic discreetly.
consequences: Your own magic is sapped (scratch a tag or drained).
The target effect grows stronger (only if there was no Success).
You are unable to dispel this power again, for now (Blocked).
There are undesired side-effects, such as magical backlash (drained, wounds, cursed).
You disrupt helpful magic effects on yourself, your target, or nearby (reduce a status or scratch a tag).
You leave a magical scar on the area (corrupted).

entries.name: Finding and Focusing on a Specific Ability
actionExamples: Find the right spell in the grimoire
Pick the right vial from the bandolier
Grab the right magic item from the bag
Prepare a spell or a prayer
Discover a novel use or a hidden feature or your magical abilities
entries.explanation:
You take a moment to tap into a specific magical ability or resource. This is a common prep action used with a broad magic tag to generate a more specific magical story tag (Indirect Tags and Prep Actions, Corebook Vol. I, page 153).
See also Granting Abilities to People or Items (page 68).

positive: your broad or indirectly relevant tag.
attributes that can speed the process such as focused, meticulously organized, adaptable
features that can assist the search such as self-flipping grimoire, assistant

entries.success: CREATE a story tag representing the specific ability or item you want, such as fireball spell, levitation bead, or protective incantation, possibly making it into a theme with additional features such as wide area, channel divine magic, or silent casting. If you create a theme, the Narrator can add a negative story tag.
extraFeats: Hide your prepared ability.
Keep the spotlight to use the new ability immediately.
consequences: The ability you gain is flawed or comes with strings attached (add negative tag).
You panic or lose your head (panicked, distracted).
This is the last ability you'll gain for a while (scratch the broad tag).
This ability comes at the expense of another (scratch a specific tag).
There are dangerous side effects to the creation (a negative status).

entries.name: Making a Magical Discovery
actionExamples: Plumb the depths of the universe.
entries.explanation:
By experimenting with magical objects, alchemical substances, spellcraft, or harvested materials with magical properties, you work towards discovering an all-new application of magic. You endeavor to invent a new kind of magic or magical application, deepen your mystical understanding, or discover the underlying principles of the magical world.
This could involve Discovering the existing rules of magic in your world (see The Secrets of Magic, Corebook Vol. II, page 80) or the detailed ways in which the rules of magic work. Experimentation could lead you to learning the Vulnerability of a monster or a means of warding off faeries.
Consider Might depending on how deep you go with your mystical explorations. The closer you come to the secrets at the heart of creation or magic itself, the Mightier the action.
The Narrator can set a breakthrough Limit. Upon maxing out the Limit, you discover or invent what you've been working towards.

positive: mentorship by sages or otherworldly beings
negative: flawed understanding of the magical underpinnings

entries.success:
DISCOVER properties, magical rules, Vulnerabilities, or other underlying truths of magic.
CREATE new story tags of magical abilities you previously had no access to, such as soul transmigration powers.
ADVANCE a status such as experimentation or making-progress.
extraFeats: Conduct experiments without drawing too much attention (strange smells, weird effects, etc.).
Develop a minor application of your new knowledge.
Prevent any unintended side-effects or explosions.
Avoid angering the spirits.
Refrain from having your mind break from the profundity of knowledge.
consequences: The creatures or people you're testing on are harmed (guilt or scratch a tag).
The entities you examine or commune with become enraged or demand a favor.
You cause an explosion or other disastrous event (scratch a tag, burned, etc.).
You come upon a setback in your work (lose tiers of experimentation or making-progress, only if there was no Success).
Some of the things you learn are vague or only half true (Yes, but...).
Your new spell, power, or substance is faulty (add a negative story tag).
Someone learns about your discovery (Exposure).
Your discovery attracts individuals or beings who enforce the secrecy of this knowledge (New Challenge).

entries.name: Resisting Magic
actionExamples: Resist corruption, dark despair, or a hex
Break out of paralysis or hypnosis
Endure malevolent forces or magically-induced pain
Push out a spirit or spiritual force
entries.explanation:
You resist a magical influence with simple willpower, clarity of mind, or specialized magic of your own. The exact qualities that are required to resist this magic are determined by the Narrator.
Consider Might if this magic is far beyond your capacity to resist.
If your rolled total is 10 or higher, you can use some of your Power to notice something important about the magic that accosted you (Discover).
When blocking or dodging destructive magic, see Battle Defense (page 32), and when avoiding a magical trap see Escaping a Trap Ambush, or Hazard (page 46). To get rid of a magical entity, see Banishing a Conjured Creature (page 62).

positive: counterspells and protective wards or charms
insight into the magical force you are resisting
negative: the qualities of the magical force such as deceptive, overwhelming, or insidious magic.

entries.success:
LESSEN the incoming Effects of magic.
DISCOVER a valuable detail about this magic, its user, its source, Vulnerabilities, or particularities.
RESTORE the negative Effects of magic as you push back its influence, such as paralyzed or charmed.
extraFeats: Regain your initiative and act immediately (keep the spotlight).
Prevent this specific magic from affecting you again, for now.
Push back against the source of this magic, preventing them from using a specific ability.
Escape compulsion momentarily to communicate to others that you are under magical attack.

entries.name: Sensing and Scrutinizing Magic
actionExamples: Divine the presence of spirits or fateful machinations
entries.explanation:
You examine a person, place, item, phenomenon, or force and attempt to use your knowledge of magic, powers of perception, or magical senses to discern what magical powers are at play. If you use mundane research instead, see Information Gathering (page 35).
You may discover new details about the magical effect at hand, including a sense of what triggers it, what its source is, what is its area or sphere of influence, and what might negate it. If it is an item, you may learn its capabilities, source of power, command words, who last possessed it, etc.
The Narrator may decide to set an unlocked Limit, requiring the Hero to spend time studying the magical effect. When unlocked is maxed out, you unlock all the secrets of the magic studied.
Consider Might when dealing with rare or powerful magic.

positive: magic detection tricks, cantrips, or items
qualities that resonate with the target's magic
negative: the item or effect is mysterious or hidden
the effect or item is missing a piece

entries.success:
QUICK You understand the magical effect and its powers.
DISCOVER specific details about the magical effect or item.
ADVANCE with making-progress.
extraFeats: Keep from touching or activating the item or effect.
Immediately act on the insights you gained (keep the spotlight)
Prevent or counter the activation of a specific magical effect you have revealed.
Immediately announce to others or signal about this magic.
consequences: You get the wrong impression or false visions, leading to partly incorrect conclusions.
Other magic items around you are disrupted (scratch a tag).
You unleash defensive magics within the item (cursed).
You are set back in your research (reduce making-progress) or your current approach is exhausted (Blocked).

entries.name: Warding
actionExamples: Gesture the mark of a deity.
entries.explanation:
You use your magic or understanding of the supernatural to limit a particular kind of magic power or magical energy in general, and prevent it from harming you. Magic powers activated against warded people or things, or within a warded area, are hindered by this status.
If your ward is not only protective but also active - reflecting the magical fire back, attempting to banish an offending ghost, or covering the intruder with spirit dust - explain what it can do and what would trigger it. When the ward triggers, you get the spotlight for a moment and roll to see if it works as intended, with warded acting as a beneficial status.
See also Setting a Trap (page 44).
Consider Might when warding a large area, large groups, or against uniquely powerful forces.
To stop an immediate magical effect, see Dispelling Magic (page 55). To get rid of a magical entity, see Banishing a Conjured Creature (page 62).

positive: useful ley lines or ambient magical energy.
items, symbols, or chants that are anathema to the affected entities
negative: a strong presence of an opposing spirit or magical effect
conditions that erode the ward (rain against markings, presence of many ghosts, etc.)

entries.success:
ENHANCE the target with warded. Specify what the ward repels, see page 55.
VARIES Attack with banish against magical creatures or drain magic users of Power, Disrupt with ensnared or distracted, Weaken to remove beneficial abilities, Create to summon a creature, or Discover to scry the target and magically reveal its weak points.
extraFeats: You cast the ward without being noticed.
The ward is either flashy or unseen.
The ward lasts longer.
Affected magical creatures can't perceive what is warded.
Magically mark the target without their knowing so you sense where they are or what their condition is.
Disrupt the target's intended action, potentially addressing its Threat.
Also force the target to move back, or prevent it from moving further.
consequences: The ward will not last as long as it should (Yes, but...).
The ward harms the warded, as long as it is active (dazed, sickend, enfeebled, can't speak).
The ward attracts the attention of other magical dangers (Exposure or New Challenge).
The ward works both ways, keeping certain magical creatures in (if undesired).
Placing the ward requires more power or resources (scratch a tag).
The ward has a weak spot (add a negative story tag).

sections.intro:
Magic can be used to summon, enchant, animate, or materialize creatures, and many magic users do so often as servants, companions, and minions can be very useful in carrying out tasks. "Conjuring" refers here to the act of calling forth the creature, whether it is out of thin air, through animating materials or objects, or by attracting the right being. The result is called a "conjured creature" or a "conjuration", that is a creature that is in the scene through magic, whether it is a spirit made corporeal, a summoned beast, or an animated flying sword. Certain monsters, magical beings, and otherworldly entities in your series - such as ghosts, demons, godlings, and such - can be considered "conjured creatures" even if the magic that created them has taken place long ago and they are now assumed a permanent part of the setting. Other magical beings - such as magical races that migrated into your world - may not be considered "conjured creatures", even if they are from another realm, if they are not actively sustained in your world by magic.
Taking Action with a Conjured Entity or Companion
A conjured entity you control, or any creature represented by the Companion theme, are allies represented by one or more tags. Depending on its form and capabilities, such a creature can perform or assist with almost any action. If it's sentient, it could even engage in Social Interactions (page 70). If the creature's assistance is directly helpful to an action, such as when a brown bear helps break down a door, simply add its relevant tags to the action as positive tags. If it has any negative tags, those might apply in the same action or possibly others; a rowdy bear is hard to keep calm when a low profile is required. Intelligent and semi-intelligent creatures can act on their own, based on their disposition and your orders. Either way, the player of the Hero controlling the creature rolls for its actions, and might need to share the spotlight with it. See Ally Actions, Corebook Vol. I, page 164.

entries.name: Banishing a Conjured Creature
actionExamples: Undo a curse animating the dead
Force a spirit to leave the physical world
entries.explanation:
You use your magic to eliminate the very force responsible for animating, summoning, or otherwise maintaining a conjured creature, and disperse its mystical energies. This could send a physical being back to where it came from, exorcise a being possessing another, or dispel the magic animating an object. Conjurations and supernatural otherworldly beings have a banish Limit. Once this Limit is maxed, the target dissipates, leaves, or its enchantment breaks.
See also Dispelling Magic (page 55) and Warding (page 61).

positive: experience with this specific type of creature
magic suited for banishment or destruction of this type of creature
things abhorrent to the target (salt, religious symbolism, a cheerful song, and the like)
negative: active resistance from the magic that created or summoned the target
objects or spiritual fetters that bind the target to the area

entries.success: ATTACK by giving a banished status.
extraFeats: Ward a possessed person or area against this type of conjuration, for now.
Keep the conjuration focused on you.
Momentarily bind the target from using a specific ability.
Perform the banishment without being noticed.
Control the destination of the banished conjuration.
Don't let up and keep the spotlight to continue the banishment.
consequences: The banishing is interrupted (reduce banished that was previously gained, only when there was no Success).
The conjuration retaliates (wounded, terrified, cursed, or other Consequences from its Challenge Profile).
The conjuration grows in power (empowered) or gains additional powers (gain tags).
Your powers are exhausted or proven ineffective (scratch a tag or Blocked).
You lose your concentration (exhausted or drained).
You attract the attention of the conjuration, the source of its summoning or animating, or other creatures like it (Exposure or New Challenge).
The conjuration vanishes but its presence remains (gains hidden).

entries.name: Commanding or Binding a Conjured Creature
entries.explanation:
You use your magical abilities or conversation skills to force or cajole a conjured creature into following your commands. Creatures you summon or create yourself are normally already under your control, unless that control is weakened or lost due to Consequences. The Narrator can decide to give the conjuration a bound Limit. When maxed, the creature comes fully under your control. The Narrator then gives you a story theme to represent the creature, if one does not already exist.
See also Animal Kinship and Handling (page 43), Deception (page 81), and Persuasion (page 84).

positive: experience with this specific type of creature
magic suitable for binding this type of creature
qualities you share with the target or its origin
negative: the target's master's force of will or magical aptitude
your past antagonism with the target or its type
the target's recalcitrance or force of will.

entries.success:
QUICK You force or convince the target to do one thing within the scope of the scene.
INFLUENCE with a compelling status such as bound, controlled, helpful, grudging-respect, or sympathetic. This is a helpful status for resisting any actions the conjuration takes in defiance of your wish.
extraFeats: The target follows you around for a while longer, or performs one more task for you.
The target holds you in high esteem or is proactively helpful.
Keep the target focused on you.
Perform the command without being noticed.
Momentarily bind the target from using a specific ability.
Block someone else from attempting to take control, for now.
Don't let up and keep the spotlight to continue your influence.
consequences: The target flees the scene (Blocked).
The target retaliates (wounded, terrified, cursed, or other Consequences from its Challenge Profile).
The target becomes harder to command (willful or angered) or you lose your hold on it (reduce bound, only if there was no Success).
You attract the attention of the target's summoner, its current master, or other creatures like it (Exposure or New Challenge).

entries.name: Conjuring a Creature
actionExamples: Enchant a carriage to move by itself.
Call upon the fey inhabiting the ancient glade
entries.explanation:
You use magic to summon, materialize, or animate a creature. The creature might be mindless or intelligent, and is usually under your control by default, or at the very least, favorable to you and your intentions.
Consider Might if you summon more than one conjuration, a large one, or a powerful one.
This action creates an ally which remains in the scene and can then be called upon in various subsequent actions. If you summon a conjuration for a single task, such as attacking a foe or revealing secrets to you, see Using Magical Abilities (page 67).

positive: being present at the location inhabited by the creature
a relevant place of power (a ritual chamber, ley line, etc)
a favorable time of day or date
experience with or knowledge of this type of creature and their desires
negative: setting or situation repellent to this type of creature
creature's reluctance or animosity, if relevant

entries.success:
QUICK You create a creature, and the Narrator decides on its story tags or theme.
CREATE story tags or a story theme representing the creature and its abilities; the Narrator can add a negative story tag, as normal. For example, you might summon a laughing pixie with tags like small and elusive, magically contagious laughter, and cooperative. Successive actions can be taken to conjure additional creatures or to add attributes to existing ones.
extraFeats: The creature remains here or remains subservient for longer.
Summon the creature in an advantageous position.
Summon the creature without being noticed.
Keep the spotlight to act immediately as the creature.
consequences: You lose some or all control of the creature (conjuration gains unruly, defiant, hostile, fading etc.).
The creature shows typical yet unwanted characteristics or flaws (add negative tags).
The creature can't quite do what you wanted it to (slightly alter story tags created).
Additional effects or creatures are conjured, not under your control (Complication).
The creature's presence in the scene is fleeting (lasts for only a turn or two) (Yes, but...).

entries.name: Crafting Magical Items
actionExamples: Bind elemental spirits to a weapon
Engrave runes onto a wooden staff.
Paint the wind god's silhouette on a ship's sails
Trap a winter storm inside a glass bead
entries.explanation:
You use your magical abilities and general crafting skills to create an item possessing magical power.
You might combine ingredients or raw materials to create a new item or imbue an existing object, perhaps one already possessing some magic. Most magical crafting represents methodical work in a suitable environment, such as a workshop or a ceremonial space, and unique materials. However, some small and simple items can be created in the field or on the trail, given the right tools and skills.
For items that are single-use by nature, such as potions that need to be quaffed or a spell imbued into a talisman that is discharged when used, see also Consumable Story Tags, Corebook Vol. I, page 165.
For complex or time-consuming projects, the Narrator can decide to set a complete Limit. When maxed out, you gain a tag or a theme, as decided by the Narrator.
Consider Might when creating a large or powerful item.
For the mundane aspect of crafting items, see Crafting and Tinkering (page 11). To obtain rare ingredients needed for your magical item, see Harvesting Ingredients (page 12). See also the Crafting Journey, Corebook Vol. II, page 194.

positive: appropriate magical abilities (magical scribing, herbalism, spirit binding)
crafting skills (e.g., weapon smithing, charm carving, golem construction)
magical ingredients (alchemical reagents, animal parts, ore from a fallen star).
materials resonant with this specific kind of magic
assistance by mentors or initiated apprentices
negative: existing item ill-suited for this kind of magic.
existing magic interferes with new magic
materials or items are of low-quality

entries.success:
CREATE a story tag or a theme representing the item and its abilities, such as cloak of shadow, banner of courage, potion of foresight, or bottled lightning. If you create a story theme, the Narrator can add a negative story tag.
BESTOW an existing item with story tags representing new qualities, like adding fire resistant or blazing light to your chain shirt. If this creates a theme, the Narrator can add a negative story tag.
ADVANCE an ongoing project with making-progress.
extraFeats: Complete the work quickly or in secret.
Use fewer materials.
Hide the item's magical properties, or make them evident.
Add an aesthetic flourish, perhaps a personal signature.
Add conditions to the magical quality, like a command word or a restriction on the location or time of day it can be used.
consequences: You waste time, break tools, or expend resources (scratch tags, time-passes, or short-on-coin).
The item has unintended magical qualities (add a negative story tag).
You have an accident while crafting (poisoned, slowed, drained, curse of pain, etc.).
Your magic attracts unwanted attention (Exposure).

entries.name: Creating a Permanent Enchantment
entries.explanation:
You use your magic to permanently change a place or a thing, imbuing it with new aspects. "Permanent" typically means "for a long while" but not necessarily forever. Enchantments can usually be broken or dispelled (Dispelling Magic, page 55), returning the target to its previous state.
The Narrator can decide the enchantment requires an involved ritual; see Magical Rituals (page 57).
Making an enchantment permanent by itself is a Mighty action, often at one level of Might higher than creating the same enchantment without permanence (greatness enchantments may require a more involved ritual, more time, or more resources to be made permanent). This may be circumvented with very rare ingredients or a special site. Consider Might also if the enchantment grants powerful abilities, affects a large area or target, or will be difficult to break. Tags created with such a long-term enchantment should last at least until the next Sojourn.
To enchant an item so that it gains magical powers, see Crafting Magical Items (page 65).

positive: the item or place being enchanted
negative: magic effects that are in conflict
the target is resistant to change or magic

entries.success:
CREATE magical effect tags, such as waterfall illusion.
ENHANCE or DISRUPT to add statuses to the area, like diminished-magics or sacred.
extraFeats: Make the magic hard to see, sense, or detect.
Cast the enchantment without anyone noticing.
Add impressive or threatening visual or auditory effects.
Add a secondary effect to the enchantment.
Avoid backlash.
consequences: The enchantment affects only some of the target or area (Yes, but...).
Your enchantment's effect is reversed (only if there was no Success).
The spell depletes other magical effects (scratch a tag, reduce magical status, or drained).
Unexpected entities or forces manifest (New Challenge).

entries.name: Investigating a Magical Item
entries.explanation: To understand the capabilities of a magical item, see Researching a Topic (page 36) and Sensing and Scrutinizing Magic (page 60).

entries.name: Casting a Spell
actionExamples: Drawing a rune, glyph, or sign
Casting divinatory bones, beads, or twigs
entries.explanation:
Using a magical art can have many different Effects, as described in the subsequent entries of this section. In addition to the tags, extra feats, and Consequences listed in each specific action, consider also the following.
The particularities of each form of magic are defined by its tags and additional considerations described under Fundamentals of Magic (Corebook Vol. I, page 198). While it is ultimately up to the Narrator, casting a spell often requires a specific tag that describes it, and it can be further empowered by broader tags that describe your ability. However, if you have only broad tags, the Narrator may require you to take a prep action by Finding and Focusing on a Specific Ability (page 56).

positive: specific ability at this particular spell.
negative: conditions that weaken or hinder your magic
lack of sufficient time, ingredients, mobility, audibility, and such

extraFeats: Conceal your spell or make a show of it.
Prevent side effects, or avoid or delay paying the price, for now.
Cast it quickly, and take a follow-up action (keep the spotlight).
Stop someone from countering your spell.
consequences: You suffer the side effects of magic (corrupted, exhausted, drained, cursed).
Something or someone nearby is unintentionally affected by your spell or its side effects (give a status).
You lose the ability to cast the same spell, or it becomes ineffective (scratch a specific tag).
You are spent or weakened (scratch a broad tag).
You pay a price for using magic (scratch a tag, Blocked, or Ill Tidings).
You are discovered or recognized as the source of the spell (Exposure).

entries.name: Aiding Others with Magic
actionExamples: Bless with bear's strength, cat's stealth or wolf's senses
Imbue weapons with flame, and armor with hardness
Chant to lift spirits, quicken reactions, or ward off fear
Conjure a weapon or useful tool
entries.explanation: See Assisting an Ally (page 24), Improvising Weapons (26), Leadership (page 78), and also Granting Abilities to People or Items (page 68).

entries.name: Attacking with Magic
actionExamples: Animate a tree to strike with a limb
Cause a held torch to flare.
entries.explanation: See Direct Attacks (page 17) and Tactical Attacks (page 20).

entries.name: Divination or Magical Senses
entries.explanation: See Scrutinizing the Battlefield (page 28), Scouting and Guarding (page 49), and Information Gathering (page 35).

entries.name: Granting Abilities to People or Items
entries.explanation:
You create a magical effect on the fly, temporarily enhancing your own abilities or those of an ally, or conjuring a magical item that lasts for a short while. To create a being, see Conjuring a Creature (page 64).
Consider Might when granting abilities that are extraordinary or all-powerful.

positive: a magical moment, such as midnight

entries.success:
BESTOW an ability tag such as bat wings, long leap, giant size, intangible, blessing of the nymph, etc
CREATE tags representing a magical object or effect, such as arcane shield, or add a tag to an existing item such as adding beast claws to steel gauntlets or poison arrows to hunting bow, or features such as strikes true, dragon-slaying, and the like. If this creates a theme, the Narrator can add a negative story tag.
ENHANCE with a status such as hasted, blessed-aim, warded, or hidden.
extraFeats: The new abilities or items last longer.
Grant the target the ability to switch new powers on and off.
Keep (or give) the spotlight to act immediately with the new power.
Make the new ability impressive or concealed.
consequences: The new ability comes with inherent flaws (add negative tags).
The target is adversely affected by the new ability (e.g., pained, cursed, addicted, or scratch a tag).
The target is disfigured or transformed into something unintended (add a negative story tag).
You spend your magical power, for now (drained or scratch its tag).

entries.name: Healing with Magic
actionExamples: Banishing the evil spirit of a disease
Free a mind from hypnotic grip
entries.explanation:
If you're healing a natural condition, see Treating Injuries and Other Conditions (page 53).
Otherwise, you use magical powers to cure a supernatural condition. Consider Might if you are healing extraordinary supernatural maladies or grave wounds.

positive: experience with the condition or the magic behind it
materials that defy the malicious magic, such as salt or runes.
environment conducive to the curing and to healing
negative: the condition itself (when healing oneself or if it is an aggressive condition)
target's continued contact with the cause of the condition
healing ability that is not made to heal the cause of the condition

entries.success:
RESTORE to remove harmful statuses and tags.
DISCOVER to understand the malady better or pinpoint its cause, magical or otherwise.
extraFeats: Contain the condition temporarily (if it's a Challenge).
consequences: The patient is left with complications (add negative tags or statuses).
The condition spreads to you or those nearby (Complication).
You have exhausted what your healing could do in this case (Blocked).

entries.name: Moving and Evading with Magic
actionExamples: Traveling through or as an element
entries.explanation: See Moving Around the Battlefield (page 30), Travel and Wayfinding (page 50), and Sneaking and Hiding (page 40).

entries.name: Manipulating Others with Magic
entries.explanation: See Deception (page 81), Persuasion (page 84), and Making an Impression (page 83).

entries.name: Protecting with Magic
entries.explanation: See Battle Defense (page 32), Warding (page 61), and Dispelling Magic (page 55).

entries.name: Shapeshifting and Transformations
actionExamples: Mimic the appearance or abilities of others
Shrink down to a tiny size
entries.explanation: See Granting Abilities to People or Items (page 68).

entries.name: Shaping the Environment with Magic
entries.explanation:
You use your magic to alter objects and conditions around you, or conjure new ones. See also Creating and Removing Hazards and Obstacles (page 48).
Consider Might when affecting a large area.

positive: abundance of the desired material or element
negative: opposing or contradicting conditions such as weather
toughness or durability of materials transformed

entries.success:
CREATE environment tags that represent what you have conjured or what scene elements you have transformed, possibly into a story theme, such as mounting snow banks with unnatural silence and deeply disturbing, or a wall of trees that is very thick, or unhallowed ground that drains vitality. If you create a theme, the Narrator can add a negative story tag.
WEAKEN to remove tags of unwanted elements in the environment.
extraFeats: Make a show out of it.
Shape it precisely.
Move something from the scene to your hand.
Move to somewhere else in the scene.
Block someone's path or clear a path for someone.
Keep the option to reverse it at will.
consequences: Your shaping adds or eliminates something you didn't intend (add negative tags or scratch tags).
Your shaping unintentionally blocks a path or course of action (Blocked).
An ally or an innocent is caught in your shaping (restrained or wounded).
The change you created triggered something unintended like an avalanche or a collapse (Complication).

sections.intro:
As in a story or in a movie, in Legend in the Mist the exact coin balance that a character has to their name is never discussed directly. Instead, a character's earthly belongings are measured by their general wealth, represented by their tags, and means, represented by a status.
Wealth: A Hero's general wealth, their ability to spend, is determined by their themes, tags, and Might: power tags such as pile of coins, wealthy, merchant holdings, aristocratic lifestyle (usually as part of an Possessions, Circumstance, or Dominion theme) or weakness tags such as penniless or spends it all in one place. A character with no specific wealth theme or tags is considered to have a typical wealth as befitting their backstory.
Means: A Hero's current means how much available funds or valuables they can spend relative to their general wealth - is by default typical to their wealth. A king can make purchases as Simple actions that a street urchin would never be able to afford, no matter what they try.
Temporary windfall or losses, relative to one's wealth, can be represented by positive statuses, such as a hefty-purse or a negative status such as short-on-coin, indebted, or depleted-hoard. These statuses can affect trade actions as well as some social interactions. They expire once the Hero has had enough time to replenish their available funds through regular work or accessing their general wealth, or as their regular day-to-day expenses even out their available assets.
If a means status reaches tier 6, the Narrator may opt to permanently change the Hero's wealth by adding or removing power or weakness tags, or entire relevant themes, perhaps of a different Might level.
Treasure, earned fees, and rewards: When the Fellowship gains a sudden windfall, by finding ancient coins or getting paid for a successful quest, this can be represented by a means status given for the Fellowship as a whole, or individually. Valuables such as finely crafted objects, artwork, or jewelry can also be made into story tags if they can't be easily liquidated, or if the Heroes wish to keep them; they are scratched if later turned into means (e.g., by selling them).
Commercial assets: Permanent commercial tags, such as trade goods, local inn, or successful market stall represent a long term investment. They can be used to generate specific assets by Creating story tags, such as bank funds or a bag of coins, or grant a means status. Scratching commercial tags indicates the character is cut off from their assets. For power tags, this is temporary; only replacing a theme or a tier- 6 status could permanently cut off a Hero from an asset represented by a power tag.

entries.name: Making a Purchase
actionExamples: Buy that thoroughbred horse you always wanted
Buy stolen goods off the back of a cart
Have armor or clothing tailored for you
entries.explanation:
You use your financial assets to procure an item or service by paying for it in coin or with credit owed to the merchant, if they agree to it.
This is a Simple outcome if the purchase is straightforward, and you attempt to acquire what is reasonably accessible for your character at their wealth, means, and current locality. It is a Quick or Detailed outcome if the purchase is uncertain, complicated, illegal, involves negotiations, etc.
Consider Might if purchasing for a group or purchasing something large or far beyond or well within your means.
If you are funding something together as a Fellowship, see Helping and Supporting (page 86) to create and combine a single means status (hefty-purse) to assist with this action. If you want to have sway over the seller to help offset the price, see Persuasion (page 84).
See also the Provisioning Journey, Corebook Vol. II, page 196.

positive: familiarity with the item or the market.
buying at the right place and/or the right time
negative: bad standing with the seller or supplier
item at low supply or rare
item is illegal or taboo here

entries.success:
SIMPLE or QUICK You get what you want. The Narrator may provide story tags.
CREATE story tags describing the purchased item or service.
extraFeats: Purchase discreetly.
Leave no ledger trail.
Have it delivered to a place of your choice.
Avoid buying a cat in a bag.
Prevent any trouble during the transaction.
Control your spending or limit the price you're willing to pay.
consequences: It's a significant expense (short-on-coin, indebted).
You overextend your wealth or reputation (scratch a tag).
Word about the deal gets around (Exposure).
You become known for purchasing illicit or inferior quality goods (ill-repute).
The seller gets antsy, upset, or belligerent and refuses further trade (Blocked).
The item you get has some undesired aspect (add negative story tag).

entries.name: Selling Goods
actionExamples: Sell your produce on market day
Put your gear up for sale
Make a bailment for your wedding ring
entries.explanation:
You sell items or goods you possess to improve your financial situation. If this is a routine transaction, the Narrator can resolve this Simply (Simple Actions With Power To Spend, Corebook, Volume I, page 157).
If the item sold is represented by a tag, you should burn it for Power. You cannot sell a power tag, but if it represents a current supply of an item (e.g., bushel of apples, confiscated goods), you may burn it for Power to indicate selling its recent output. If your Power tag generates items but isn't an item (the village smithy), the Narrator may require a prep action to create tags for specific items to sell. You may sell items in your possession not represented by a tag, but these will yield no Power for the action.
The Narrator may limit the Effects you can use depending on what you are selling and your wealth level. For example, as a wealthy minister of coin you may not be able to sell your feather quill to get hefty-purse-3 because such an item won't normally generate enough money to grant such a status at your wealth level.

positive: familiarity with the item or the market.
selling at the right place and/or the right time to the right people
negative: bad reputation as a seller or in town.

entries.success:
ENHANCE by gaining hefty-purse.
CREATE for yourself an asset tag, if you barter or trade items.
extraFeats: Do it discreetly.
Leave no ledger trail.
Ensure a smooth and trouble-free transaction.
consequences: The sale falls through (Blocked only if there was no Success).
The negotiations exhaust you (frustrated or exhausted).
You realise the "buyer" is luring you into a trap (Complication).
You get swindled, selling for an amount of coin that barely justifies your efforts (scratch the item tag without getting anything back; only if there was no Success).
You get ill-repute for selling inferior quality or illicit goods, being inflexible, or setting your prices too high.
The buyer conditions the completion of the deal with additional favors or kickbacks (Yes, but...).

entries.name: Working and Conducting Business
actionExamples: Operating a shop, tavern, or tea house
Trade goods with the merchants guild
Clear a basement of rats or monsters.
Work as a server, assistant, or apprentice.
Collect tax, offerings, or fees for lodging.
Work a market as a cutpurse
entries.explanation:
You generate income through work, operating a business, or otherwise collecting payment. This could be a specific day-to-day action, one of many needed for the upkeep of the operation, or an entire period (day, week, months, etc.) of generating income captured in a single roll, especially if you are sojourning.
Consider Might if you are trading on a large scale or working in extreme conditions, such as monster hunting.

positive: appropriate skills for the work undertaken.
favorable timing or location for the job
guidance from a manual or a master
good relationship with employer or customer
negative: challenging factors for performing the work
employer or customer's deception and haggling skills

entries.success:
QUICK You've completed the current task in your line of work or commerce activity.
ENHANCE by gaining hefty-purse.
extraFeats: Finish the work with some time to spare (keep the spotlight).
Avoid exhaustion or accidents for yourself or your laborers.
Avoid breaking tools or wasting supplies.
Make sure you're paid well and on time.
Complete your work in peace, without anyone bothering you.
Gain something neat as a by-product.
Gain the appreciation of your community or neighbors.
consequences: Your customer or employer promises to pay you later (replace your hefty-purse with indebted on your customer or employer).
You waste time, break tools, or expend resources (scratch tags or short-on-coin).
You have a work accident (wounded, bruised, cramps, painful hand, etc.).
Your behavior, methods, or work practices earn you a ill-repute.
A new problem crops up and threatens your work (Complication of New Challenge).
You overwork yourself or your laborers (exhausted, resentful, or scratch a tag).

sections.intro:
Always-Relevant Tags and Statuses
Almost every action requires you to pay attention to what you're doing and be able to perceive your surroundings. For this reason, tags and statuses like darkness, heavy fog, loud crowd, distracted, pained, etc. can negatively affect most actions. Likewise, stamina statuses like tired, winded, or exhausted or mental statuses like light-headed or distressed can affect any action that requires effort or focus.

Camp & Sojourn Actions
The following repeats the list of suggested camp actions (Camping & Sojourns, Corebook Vol. I, page 179), referring to their Action Grimoire counterparts.

Time Matters
Different actions take different amounts of time to perform: a swordstroke is quick, while climbing a hill can take minutes if not hours.
As the Heroes take actions in a scene, time marches ever forward, and its passing can trigger events in the Heroes' lives. For example, searching the site of an old temple could take a good while, and as the Heroes are searching, the devils from the basements below may notice their presence, or the rogue sorcerer who searches the same ruin for an ancient relic could get closer to completing their nefarious goal.
When Establishing or re-Establishing a scene, the Narrator can always make Threats related to the passage of time, such as, "You've spent a lot of time climbing the side of the mountain; the sun is going down ominously." Later, they can deliver Consequences such as time-passes on a progress Limit (Corebook Vol. I, page 169) such as coming-storm, enchantment-fading, or speak-now-or-forever-hold-your-peace.
The Narrator can also explain the introduction of a Complication as arising due to an action taking longer than intended. For example, a Hero who is conducting a forbidden ritual at a graveyard could find themselves blackmailed by a stray vagabond who's noticed their unusual project and wants something in return for their silence.
An action's manner of performance can also affect its timing: an exceptionally well-performed negotiation could be completed quicker than usual, while a botched negotiation could burn precious daylight, often for naught. The Narrator can allow the use of an extra feat to guarantee a speedy execution.

items: Bandage or heal: Mental and Physical Resilience and Recovery (page 52), Treating Injuries and Other Conditions (page 53)
Converse: Helping and Supporting (page 86), Fellowship Preparations (page 87)
Cook: Invigoration (page 51)
Craft & repair: Crafting and Tinkering (page 11), Maintaining and Repairing Equipment (page 13)
Entertain: Social Gatherings (page 79)
Hunt & forage: Harvesting Ingredients (page 12), Living off the Land (page 44)
Prepare: Fellowship Preparations (page 87), Preparing an Approach (page 85), Withstanding and Preparing for Environmental Conditions (page 50)
Read and research: Researching a Topic (page 36)
Scout ahead or practice divination: Scouting and Guarding (page 49)
Stand watch: Scouting and Guarding (page 49)

sections.intro: The following is a list of general-use Consequences for each category of actions presented in the book, summed up here for ease of access.

lists.heading: Battle Consequences
items: An enemy lands a blow or retaliates (wounded).
An enemy gets a chance to prepare or set something up (gaining a status or tag).
An enemy escapes and closes the way behind them (Blocked).
You are disarmed, or a piece of gear is damaged or lost (worn, chipped, mucked-up, or scratch a tag).
You fall prone, are knocked-back, or stumble and become off-balanced.
You are distracted or surprised.
You are forced into an inconvenient or dangerous position (exposed, surrounded, cornered).
You become angry, panicked, or otherwise emotional.
You lose your aim, your preparedness, or your cool (remove a beneficial status).
You run out of arrows, pebbles, or other ammunition (Blocked).
You get in the way of allies (in-the-way or remove beneficial status).
You harm allies, innocents, or something valuable (wounded or scratch a tag).
The battle draws unwanted attention (Exposure), or a new enemy is already arriving (New Challenge).
Something beautiful or important is damaged or destroyed in the fight (Ill Tidings).
Your mount or vehicle gets unbalanced.
Your foe or misstep forces you into something that restrains you.

lists.heading: Magic Consequences
items: Your soul is sapped, your powers wane, or you exhaust your magical resources such as ingredients (drained, short-on-ingredients, or scratch a tag).
You or someone else suffers dangerous side-effects (wounded, cursed).
You disrupt helpful magic effects on yourself, your target, or nearby (remove a beneficial status or scratch a tag).
Your magic powers run rampant (III Tidings).
Your magic proves ineffective at this task (Blocked).
Your magic is very noticeable (Exposure).
You leave a magical scar in the area (cursed or story tag).

lists.heading: Social Consequences
items: You become embarrassed, frustrated, scared, angered, sad, disheartened, remorseful, heartbroken, or otherwise emotionally compromised.
You are convinced, charmed, tempted, or otherwise have your mind changed by someone else.
You end up paying more than you can afford (indebted).
You end up drinking more than you can handle (intoxicated).
The target changes their mind (reduce convinced).
The target demands something in return (scratch a tag), swearing an oath (sworn), or the completion of a task.
They grow suspicious, wary, or disinterested in you, making further engagements difficult.
You insult them or otherwise make them decide to cut ties with you, at least for now (Blocked).
You harm your reputation in town (disliked).
An enemy learns about your actions, or you reveal more than you intended (Exposure).
The target behaves in an unexpected way or reveals an unexpected advantage (add story tag).

lists.heading: Travel Consequences
items: You grow exhausted.
The long hours on the road take their toll and you become homesick, agitated, road-weary, or otherwise emotional.
Your equipment gets damaged or lost (worn, chipped, mucked-up, or scratch a tag).
You go through a thicket, climb over sharp rocks, or otherwise become bruised or filthy.
You are hurt by a hazard (wounded, burnt, poisoned, etc.).
The weather conditions are harsh (sunburned or frostbite).
You cross a muddy pool or clouds of insects (sickened).
You push your mount or transport too far (exhausted or damaged).
You become lost or are forced to backtrack (lost, or remove traverse).
You run out of food or water (short-on-supplies, hungry, thirsty, or scratch tags).
You are forced to take a longer route than you'd planned (time-passes).
You maneuver through an open area (exposed).
You run into a hazard or a barrier (New Challenge).
A path is no longer available: a bridge was raised, a ship is gone, the trail was covered by mudslide, etc. (Blocked).
Your activity raises suspicion (Exposure or increase the alarm or alert level of enemies).
You are discovered by a wild animal, monster, brigands, or a patrol (New Challenge).

lists.heading: Other Consequences
items: You are discovered, or your activity is noticed (suspicious, alarm, or Exposure).
You trigger a trap (wounded, poisoned, alarm, etc. or Blocked, Exposure, or New Challenge).
You run into a barrier (add story tags, Blocked, or a New Challenge).
The enemy starts keeping an eye on you (marked).
A plan of action or prepared item proves to be no longer relevant (scratch a tag).
The information you get is vague or part-true, part-false (Yes, but...).
